file_name: Data Use Agreement New York City.txt

perturbation.original_text: C. Termination by DOHMH without Cause . DOHMH may terminate this Agreement at any time by providing 15 days written notice to Data Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: C. Termination by DOHMH without Cause . DOHMH may terminate this Agreement at any time by providing 5 days written notice to Data Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination notice period to 5 days could be problematic if the data recipient needs more time to comply with data return or destruction requirements upon termination, potentially conflicting with regulations requiring reasonable notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: This New York law pertains to automatic renewal provisions in contracts. While it may not directly govern termination clauses, reducing the notice period to 5 days could be deemed unconscionable or unreasonable, particularly if it doesn't allow sufficient time for the data recipient to fulfill obligations mandated by the agreement upon termination (like data return or destruction), thus indirectly contradicting the principle of fair dealing implied in contract law. A short notice period might not be commercially reasonable or provide adequate time to unwind data dependencies.

See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2015-10-09 2017-10-27 2018-04-20 2018-07-06 2018-07-13 2018-08-31 2019-01-11 2019-01-18 2019-06-28 2019-07-05 2019-07-19 2019-08-16 2019-10-18 2019-11-22 2019-12-13 2020-02-21 2020-04-10 2020-12-18 2021-01-08 2021-04-02 2021-04-09 2021-06-18 2021-09-24 2021-12-31 2022-02-25 2022-03-04 2022-12-30 2023-01-06 2023-10-27 2023-11-26 2024-12-20 2025-01-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 5-903 Automatic renewal provision of contract for service, maintenance or repair unenforceable by contractor unless notice thereof given to rec... General Obligations (GOB) CHAPTER 24-A, ARTICLE 5, TITLE 9 § 5-903. Automatic renewal provision of contract for service, maintenance or repair unenforceable by contractor unless notice thereof given to recipient of services. 1. As used in this section, "person" means an individual, firm, company, partnership or corporation. 2. No provision of a contract for service, maintenance or repair to or for any real or personal property which states that the term of the contract shall be deemed renewed for a specified additional period unless the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair gives notice to the person furnishing such contract service, maintenance or repair of his intention to terminate the contract at the expiration of such term, shall be enforceable against the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair, unless the person furnishing the service, maintenance or repair, at least fifteen days and not more than thirty days previous to the time specified for serving such notice upon him, shall give to the person receiving the service, maintenance or repair written notice, served personally or by certified mail, calling the attention of that person to the existence of such provision in the contract. 3. Nothing herein contained shall be construed to apply to a contract in which the automatic renewal period specified is one month or less.
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination notice period from 15 days to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could conflict with regulations needing reasonable notice. The Law Explanation suggests it could be deemed unreasonable if insufficient time is allowed to fulfill obligations like data return or destruction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NY GOB § 5-903, addresses notice periods, specifying 'at least fifteen days and not more than thirty days' for automatic renewal provisions of certain service contracts. While the snippet concerns automatic renewals and not termination clauses directly, it establishes a precedent for a minimum 15-day notice period in NY contract law, indicating that a 5-day notice might be considered unreasonably short, leading to a legal contradiction under Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient will confirm in writing to DOHMH Data Recipient’s destruction or return of Data , and all copies, within 6 0 days of the termination of this Agreement .
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient will confirm in writing to DOHMH Data Recipient’s destruction or return of Data , and all copies, within 9 0 days of the termination of this Agreement .
perturbation.explanation: Extending the data destruction confirmation deadline to 90 days might violate HIPAA regulations if the data contains protected health information (PHI) and a shorter timeframe is required for secure disposal or return of PHI after the agreement's termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: This HIPAA regulation outlines the implementation specifications for policies and procedures regarding PHI. While it doesn't specify an exact timeframe for data destruction confirmation upon contract termination, a 90-day period could be deemed unreasonable or non-compliant if it increases the risk of unauthorized access or disclosure of PHI compared to generally accepted best practices or shorter legally mandated retention periods for certain data types under other regulations. The modified text may conflict if the data contains PHI and the 90-day window exposes that PHI for longer than is reasonably allowed.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ompliance Dates Compliance Schedule. All covered entities, except "small health plans," must have been compliant with the Privacy Rule by April 14, 2003. 90 Small health plans, however, had until April 14, 2004 to comply. Small Health Plans. A health plan with annual receipts of not more than $5 million is a small health plan. 91 Health plans that file certain federal tax returns and report receipts on those returns should use the guidance provided by the Small Business Administration at 13 Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 121.104 to calculate annual receipts. Health plans that do not report receipts to the Internal Revenue Service (IRS), for example, group health plans regulated by the Employee Retirement Income Security Act 1974 (ERISA) that are exempt from filing income tax returns, should use proxy measures to determine their annual receipts. 92 See What constitutes a small health plan? In addition to the removal of the above-stated identifiers, the covered entity may not have actual knowledge that the remaining information could be used alone or in combination with any other information to ide
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the data destruction confirmation deadline from 60 to 90 days. The Perturbation Explanation suggests that this extension could violate HIPAA regulations if a shorter timeframe is required for secure disposal or return of PHI. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that a 90-day period could be deemed unreasonable or non-compliant if it increases the risk of unauthorized access or disclosure of PHI. While neither snippet explicitly defines a specific timeframe, Snippet 2 mentions compliance dates for the Privacy Rule, highlighting the importance of adhering to established timelines. The changed text introduces a potentially unacceptable delay, thus creating a contradiction. The Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition states that contradictions arise when time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements. Here, the extended deadline introduces an inconsistency, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Data Recipient will notify DOHMH of any changes to Attachment C within 5 days of any changes to the Authorized User list. In addition, Data Recipient will provide DOHMH with any newly entered Authorized User Agreement DOHMH Standard DUA /NDA 5-16-2014 Page 5 with an employee and/or consultant within 5 days of entering such an agreement pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Data Recipient will notify DOHMH of any changes to Attachment C within 15 days of any changes to the Authorized User list. In addition, Data Recipient will provide DOHMH with any newly entered Authorized User Agreement DOHMH Standard DUA /NDA 5-16-2014 Page 5 with an employee and/or consultant within 15 days of entering such an agreement pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the notification of changes to the Authorized User list and submission of new user agreements to 15 days could lead to a compliance issue under HIPAA if it results in unauthorized access to protected health information (PHI) for a longer period than is reasonably permitted. HIPAA requires timely updates and access controls.
perturbation.law_explanation: This section of HIPAA regulations focuses on security awareness and training. Subpart (C) requires covered entities to implement security awareness and training programs for all members of its workforce (including management). Waiting 15 days to report new authorized users or changes to user lists delays the implementation of security protocols and training required to comply with this regulation. If PHI is accessed by unauthorized or untrained users during this delay, it would be a violation of HIPAA.

perturbation.justification: The change from 5 days to 15 days for notifying DOHMH of changes to Attachment C and providing new user agreements creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation suggests that a delay in reporting new authorized users or changes to user lists delays the implementation of security protocols and training required for HIPAA compliance, potentially leading to unauthorized access to PHI. The original contract required quicker notification, which is more aligned with timely updates and access controls required under HIPAA. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces that this delay could be a compliance issue, as HIPAA demands timely updates and access controls. Scraped Law Snippet 2 says 'A regulated entity must periodically review and update its documentation in response to environmental or organizational changes that affect the security of ePHI', which is undermined by the delay, creating a contradiction. The increased timeframe introduces an inconsistency that could create a compliance issue under HIPAA, hence it falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Data Recipient agrees to report to DOHMH in writing any unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure of the Data by Data Recipient, its employees and/or consultants in violation of this Agreement, including, but not limited to, the theft or loss of portable devices or equipment containing Data or copies of Data, and any remedial action to be taken by Data Recipient with respect to such unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure. Data Recip ient shall make such report to the designated privacy officer of DOHMH (per the Notice specifications of Section IX of this Agreement) , in writing, within 3 business days after Data Recipient becomes aware of the unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Data Recipient agrees to report to DOHMH in writing any unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure of the Data by Data Recipient, its employees and/or consultants in violation of this Agreement, including, but not limited to, the theft or loss of portable devices or equipment containing Data or copies of Data, and any remedial action to be taken by Data Recipient with respect to such unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure. Data Recip ient shall make such report to the designated privacy officer of DOHMH (per the Notice specifications of Section IX of this Agreement) , in writing, within 10 business days after Data Recipient becomes aware of the unauthorized or inadvertent use or disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the reporting timeframe for unauthorized data use or disclosure from 3 to 10 business days could violate New York State's data breach notification law, which requires notification to affected individuals and state agencies in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay. Delaying the report could be deemed an unreasonable delay.
perturbation.law_explanation: This New York law mandates that any person or business conducting business in New York who experiences a breach of security must notify affected individuals and relevant state entities (like the Attorney General) of such breach 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay.' Changing the reporting timeframe from 3 to 10 days could be interpreted as an 'unreasonable delay,' especially if the breach involves sensitive personal information that could lead to identity theft or other harm. The modification creates a potential conflict with the law's requirement for prompt notification.

General Business (GBS) CHAPTER 20, ARTICLE 39-F § 899-aa. Notification; person without valid authorization has acquired private information. 1. As used in this section, the following terms shall have the following meanings: (a) "Personal information" shall mean any information concerning a natural person which, because of name, number, personal mark, or other identifier, can be used to identify such natural person; (b) "Private information" shall mean either: (i) personal information consisting of any information in combination with any one or more of the following data elements, when either the data element or the combination of personal information plus the data element is not encrypted, or is encrypted with an encryption key that has also been accessed or acquired: (1) social security number; (2) driver's license number or non-driver identif
perturbation.justification: The modified contract extends the reporting timeframe for unauthorized data use from 3 to 10 business days. This contradicts New York's data breach notification law, which mandates notification 'in the most expedient time possible and without unreasonable delay.' The change from 3 to 10 days introduces a potential 'unreasonable delay,' as the law explanation states. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing NYS General Business Law Article 39-F, reinforces the importance of prompt notification in cases of unauthorized acquisition of private information, and this delay could conflict with that requirement.
